advaita.rendering: The syllable Oṃ (oṃkāra) is the verbal designation (abhidhāna) of Brahman itself — not a pointer to something external but the name-form whose referent is the Lord (Īśvara). One who departs uttering that syllable while inwardly attending to its referent, the supreme Self, quits the body without the Self being destroyed — for it is the body that is abandoned, not the ātman, which has no form to lose. Such a one reaches the supreme state (paramā gati), here the non-return to conditioned existence that is the fruit of realized non-difference.
advaita.anchor: Śaṅkara: 'oṃkāram (the syllable Oṃ) vyāharan (uttering), tad-artha-bhūtaṃ mām (me who am its meaning) anusmaran (recollecting) … deha-tyāgena prayāṇam ātmanaḥ na svarūpa-nāśena (departure through body-abandonment, not through destruction of the Self's own nature).'
advaita.divergence: Śaṅkara reads Oṃ as the designator of the nirguṇa Brahman; 'mām' (me/Kṛṣṇa) is absorbed into Brahman-as-Īśvara — the destination is non-dual liberation, not a personal divine abode.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Having withdrawn all the senses from their objects and restrained the mind in the lotus-heart where Bhagavān Himself dwells, the devotee utters Oṃ — which here names Me directly (mad-vācaka) — and holds My image unwaveringly in consciousness. At death he propels the prāṇa (life-breath) to the crown and departs: he attains an ātman liberated from prakṛti, identical in form to Mine (mat-samānākāra), never to return. The destination is not mere cessation but the conscious bliss of dwelling in the Blessed Lord's likeness.
viśiṣṭādvaita.anchor: Rāmānuja: 'oṃ iti ekaākṣaraṃ brahma mad-vācakam (Oṃ, the single syllable Brahman, which names Me) … prakṛti-viyuktam mat-samānākāram apunar-āvṛttim ātmānam prāpnoti (he attains a self freed from prakṛti, of My own form, never to return).'
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence: Where Śaṅkara absorbs 'mām' into impersonal Brahman, Rāmānuja insists Oṃ names the personal Bhagavān; liberation is not formless dissolution but ātman in Bhagavān's own divine form (sārūpya-mukti).

dvaita.rendering: All gates of the body having been sealed, the mind fixed on Nārāyaṇa in the heart (hṛdi Nārāyaṇe), the yogin departs through the brahma-nāḍī alone — for any other channel leads to rebirth, not liberation. Where the prāṇa goes, the mind follows, and where the mind is, there the jīva stands dependent on Hari. Oṃ as the name of the eternally distinct supreme Lord seals the jīva's identity as servant, and the departure through the brahma-nāḍī is the mark of that servant's final homecoming to Hari.
dvaita.anchor: Madhva: 'brahma-nāḍīṃ vinā yad yad anyatra gacchati tarhi vinā mokṣaṃ sthānāntaraṃ prāpnoti (whoever goes other than through the brahma-nāḍī attains some other state short of liberation) … hṛdi Nārāyaṇe (Nārāyaṇa in the heart).'
dvaita.divergence: Madhva alone insists on the exclusivity of the brahma-nāḍī pathway and on eternal jīva-Brahman distinction; the jīva attains Hari's presence but never becomes Hari — servanthood (dāsatva) persists in liberation itself.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: In the Brahma-vāda Kṛṣṇa Himself is of the nature of name and form (nāma-rūpātmakatva); therefore the yogin who recollects Me in the single-syllable form Oṃ and simultaneously utters It is not deploying a technique but entering the streaming grace (prasāda) of Kṛṣṇa's own self-disclosure. The 'supreme destination' named here is precisely that padatva — the status of Kṛṣṇa's own realm — which is not achieved but received as the fruit of this total surrender at the final moment.
śuddhādvaita.anchor: Vallabha: 'brahma-vāde mamaivanāma-rūpātmakatvāt (in the Brahma-teaching, because I Myself am of the nature of name and form) … oṃ ity ekākṣara-rūpam anusmaran tathā vyāharan antakāle paramām etāṃ pada-tvena nirdiṣṭāṃ gatiṃ yāti (recollecting and uttering Oṃ, the single-syllable form, at the final moment he attains the destination designated as pada, the divine abode).'
śuddhādvaita.divergence: Vallabha collapses the gap between the name Oṃ and Kṛṣṇa's own essence: uttering Oṃ is not pointing at Brahman but is Kṛṣṇa manifesting as sound — the path is not practice but līlā-participation.

bhakti.rendering: The single syllable Oṃ is Brahman — either because it directly denotes Brahman (brahma-vācakatva) or because it serves as its emblem, as an image (pratimā) stands for the deity. Uttering this syllable while simultaneously remembering Me, its referent, and then abandoning the body, the departing soul travels by the luminous path (arcirādi-mārga) and reaches My supreme destination. The double requirement — utterance and recollection together — means neither external repetition alone nor silent meditation alone suffices.
bhakti.anchor: Śrīdhara: 'oṃ ity ekaṃ yad akṣaraṃ tad eva brahma-vācakatvād vā pratimādivad brahma-pratīkatvād vā brahma … tad-vācyaṃ ca mām anusmaran eva dehaṃ tyajan … arcirādi-mārgena sa paramāṃ śreṣṭhāṃ mad-gatiṃ yāti (uttering that, and recollecting Me its referent, abandoning the body … by the arcirādi path he attains my supreme, best destination).'
bhakti.divergence: Śrīdhara's arcirādi-mārga (the shining path of progressive liberation described in Chāndogya Upaniṣad) marks this as a mediated journey through subtle realms rather than Madhva's nāḍī-ascent or Śaṅkara's instantaneous jñāna-liberation.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Oṃ is the imperishable (akṣara), non-dual, all-pervading Brahman whose verbal body is sound; meditating on its meaning — which is Me — the yogin traverses the devayāna path to Brahmaloka and thence, when the merit of that divine experience is exhausted, attains the highest destination which is My own form (mad-rūpā paramā gati). Patañjali's own testimony confirms this: 'the praṇava is Its designator; its japa and contemplation of its meaning constitute Īśvara-praṇidhāna,' and from that praṇidhāna samādhi-siddhi arises — hence no contradiction between the yoga-śāstra and this verse.
advaita-bhakti.anchor: Madhusūdana: 'oṃ ity ekākṣaram ekam advitīyam akṣaram avināśi sarva-vyāpakaṃ brahma māṃ oṃ ity asya arthaṃ smaran … devayāna-mārgeṇa brahma-lokaṃ gatvā tad-bhogānte paramāṃ prakṛṣṭāṃ gatiṃ mad-rūpām (recollecting Me the meaning of Oṃ … going via the devayāna to Brahmaloka, and at the exhaustion of those enjoyments, the supreme destination which is My own form).'
advaita-bhakti.divergence: Madhusūdana uniquely layers the two soteriological models: first Brahmaloka-enjoyment (the Vedāntic upper path), then final dissolution into Kṛṣṇa's own form — fusing Advaita's non-dual terminus with bhakti's personal Bhagavān in a single sequential arc.

so_what_questions: Does the verse require a fully trained yogin or is the death-time utterance of Oṃ accessible to an ordinary devotee who has practiced sporadically — and what happens if the utterance is mechanical rather than attended by genuine recollection (anusmṛti)?

Śaṅkara and Rāmānuja agree that Oṃ names Brahman/Bhagavān but sharply disagree on whether the destination is formless absorption or conscious dwelling in divine form — can a single verse sustain both readings or does the grammar of 'mām anusmaran' (recollecting Me) force a personal referent?

Madhva's insistence that liberation requires departure through the brahma-nāḍī specifically raises a physiological question: is this channel a yogic-anatomical fact, a metaphorical description of upward intention, or a polemical marker separating Dvaita from other schools?

If Oṃ is simultaneously the name, the form, and the meaning of Kṛṣṇa (Vallabha's nāma-rūpātmakatva), does the verse then teach that Oṃ-utterance is itself the destination rather than a means to it — and how does that collapse the conventional sādhana-sādhya (means-end) structure?

Madhusūdana's two-stage model — Brahmaloka first, then the supreme destination — implies that even a devotee who utters Oṃ correctly may still undergo further refinement after death; does this introduce a form of purgatorial progress into an Advaita framework that otherwise denies gradual liberation?

All six schools treat the simultaneous co-presence of utterance (vyāharan) and recollection (anusmaran) as the operative formula; what is the relationship between phonetic production and mental attention — can one meaningfully utter without recollecting, and what is the failure mode if the two come apart at the moment of death?

The verse specifies 'tyajan deham (abandoning the body)' — a participle that implies voluntary relinquishment rather than passive dying; does the tradition hold that only yogins who die consciously qualify, and how do the schools handle the case of sudden or involuntary death?

advaita.application: Begin and close every deliberate activity — waking, sitting for study, entering conversation — with a single unhurried Oṃ whose sound you follow all the way into silence. The practice is not ritual but repeated rehearsal of the death-time act: resting attention on the silence that underlies the syllable, which Śaṅkara identifies as Brahman itself. Over years this makes the final moment a repetition of what has been daily life, not an emergency performance.
advaita.grounding: Anchored in Śaṅkara's 'tad-artha-bhūtaṃ mām anusmaran' — the daily practice trains the identification of Oṃ's referent as the witnessing Self (ātman), so that at death no new act is required.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: During daily sandhyāvandana (dawn/dusk worship) or pūjā, after withdrawing the senses and settling into the breath, hold the image of Bhagavān in the heart-lotus (hṛdaya-kamala) while the prāṇava is recited — treating each recitation as a rehearsal of Rāmānuja's 'mūrdhni prāṇam ādhāya' (directing the life-breath to the crown). The visible image on the altar and the internal image in the heart should be understood as the same qualified Brahman, preventing the dissociation between outer ritual and inner bhāvanā.
viśiṣṭādvaita.grounding: Rāmānuja's commentary explicitly joins the breath-at-crown technique with simultaneous mental fixing on Bhagavān — the everyday application is to never treat prāṇāyāma and Bhagavad-dhyāna as separate practices.

dvaita.application: Cultivate a daily practice of locating Nārāyaṇa specifically in the hṛdaya (heart) — not as a general 'divine presence' but as Hari, eternally distinct, whose supremacy (pāramparyam) the jīva serves. When reciting Oṃ or any Viṣṇu-nāma (name of Viṣṇu), explicitly acknowledge the separation: 'I am the servant, He is the Lord.' This counteracts the Advaita tendency to collapse the worshiper into the worshiped and maintains the devotional tension (bheda) that Madhva holds is preserved even in mokṣa.
dvaita.grounding: Madhva's 'hṛdi Nārāyaṇe' and his citation of the Vyāsa-yoga on the inseparability of prāṇa, manas, and jīva ground a practice of triune alignment: life-breath, mind, and personhood all pointing toward the same distinct Hari.

śuddhādvaita.application: In Puṣṭi-mārga, displace the performance-anxiety around 'correct' Oṃ-practice by recognizing that Kṛṣṇa is already the syllable's own nature (nāma-rūpātmakatva). Everyday service — preparing food for the svarūpa (home deity-image), singing kīrtana, telling stories of Kṛṣṇa's līlā — is therefore a continuous extension of this verse's instruction. The 'single moment at death' is simply the culmination of a life lived as ongoing Name-remembrance; no special last-moment technique is required if the whole life has been soaked in prasāda.
śuddhādvaita.grounding: Vallabha: 'mamaivanāma-rūpātmakatvāt' — because Kṛṣṇa is of the nature of name and form, every act of Name-repetition or form-service is already the practice of this verse.

bhakti.application: Pair the morning recitation of the praṇava with a concrete devotional anchor — a verse, an image, a remembered act of grace — so that Oṃ does not remain abstract. Śrīdhara's dual formula (utterance + recollection of the referent simultaneously) translates into a two-beat practice: sound on the outbreath, image or divine quality on the inbreath. Over time the gap between the two beats closes. This also addresses the arcirādi-mārga understanding: the luminous path begins now, in the quality of attention brought to the Name, not only at the moment of death.
bhakti.grounding: Śrīdhara's 'tad-vācyaṃ ca mām anusmaran eva' (recollecting Me, the referent) makes simultaneous remembrance structurally required, not optional — the everyday practice trains exactly this co-presence.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's synthesis invites a graduated daily practice: begin with structured japa of Oṃ as Patañjali's praṇidhāna (surrender-through-the-praṇava), move into bhāvanā of Kṛṣṇa as the syllable's meaning, and allow the meditation to deepen toward the non-dual silence behind both sound and image. The practitioner need not choose between the Yoga-sūtra's impersonal absorption and the Bhāgavata's personal Bhagavān; Madhusūdana's commentary authorizes holding both simultaneously, with the personal form as the entry-point and non-dual recognition as the horizon.
advaita-bhakti.grounding: Madhusūdana explicitly cites Patañjali's 'tasya vācakaḥ praṇavaḥ, taj-japas tad-artha-bhāvanam' alongside Kṛṣṇa's 'mad-rūpāṃ paramāṃ gatiṃ' — the everyday practice is precisely this two-horizon meditation, not a forced choice between the two.

self_assessment: Six bhāṣyas all present and substantive; all renderings drawn directly from supplied Devanagari prose with no gap-filling; Śrīdhara's payload was clean Sanskrit with no HTML artifacts; the Madhusūdana rendering is the most complex due to his explicit Patañjali citation chain, faithfully reproduced.
